title: Six Questions for the Seder Night, as recalled by Paul Christian Kirchner in his Jüdisches Ceremoniel (Nuremberg, 1717)
author: Isaac Gantwerk Mayer (transcription & naqdanut)

coauthors.name: Isaac Gantwerk Mayer (transcription & naqdanut)
coauthors.bio: From a family of musicians, Isaac Gantwerk Mayer believes that creative art is one of the most powerful ways to get in touch with the divine. He composes music and poetry in Hebrew and English. (He also translates and authors his own original works.) Isaac runs a Jewish music transcription service, which will transcribe and set any Jewish music in any language, recorded or written. Contact his service on Facebook or via his music blog.

coauthors.name: Isaac Gantwerk Mayer (translation)
coauthors.bio: From a family of musicians, Isaac Gantwerk Mayer believes that creative art is one of the most powerful ways to get in touch with the divine. He composes music and poetry in Hebrew and English. (He also authors his own original works and transcribes Hebrew and Aramaic text, adding niqqud and t'amim as needed.) Isaac runs a Jewish music transcription service, which will transcribe and set any Jewish music in any language, recorded or written. Contact his service on Facebook or via his music blog.

coauthors.name: Paul Christian Kirchner (translation)
coauthors.bio: Paul Christian Kirchner (born Gumpel ben Mordechai; ca. 1675-?), born in Krotszyn, Poland was an educated Jewish convert to Christianity. From a rabbinic family, he claimed to have been a rabbi for about eight years before his conversion. He was baptized in November 1713 in the Lutheran church in Bischleben in the presence of eleven godparents from the upper classes of Dutchy of Gotha and the city of Erfurt, indicating the prestigiousness of the event. Kircher then moved to Jena where he matriculated at the Lutheran university. He had hoped to earn a living as lecturer in Hebrew and was given a letter of recommendation for the University of Jena but he was unable to gain a permanent foothold in academia. The search for employment took him across Europe, from Königsberg to Basel. The work took a critical stance on Judaism but which was later republished by Sebastian Jugendres with more neutral text. It is generally assumed, although not proven, that he returned to Judaism. At least fifteen editions of his Jüdisches Ceremoniel were printed between 1716 and 1737. (From the article by Maria Diemling in the Journal of Early Modern Christianity 2025; 12(1), pp. 77-78.)

excerpt: Born into Judaism, Paul Christian Kirchner was baptized a Christian in 1713. In 1717, he first published the word "Jüdisches Ceremoniel" or "Jewish Ceremony," for which he is most well known — a firsthand recollection (and, to be honest, criticism) of Jewish practices throughout the year and life cycle. While the work is written by an apostate for a Christian audience, and thus it is certainly not an unbiased source — and it had been almost a decade since he left the community so some of his memories are a bit muddled — it is still a fascinating work if only for its depiction of Jewish practices otherwise forgotten. In this portion of Kirchner's description of the Seder night, he describes a ritual somewhat similar to the one still practiced in many North African communities to this day along with six — count 'em, six! — questions asked by the eldest (‽) child to the father.
content:
Born into Judaism, Paul Christian Kirchner was baptized a Christian in 1713. In 1717, he first published the work Jüdisches Ceremoniel (or “Jewish Ceremony”), for which he is most well known — a firsthand recollection (and, to be honest, criticism) of Jewish practices throughout the year and life cycle. While the work is written by an apostate for a Christian audience, and thus it is certainly not an unbiased source — and it had been almost a decade since he left the community so some of his memories are a bit muddled — it is still a fascinating work if only for its depiction of Jewish practices otherwise forgotten. In this portion of Kirchner’s description of the Seder night, he describes a ritual somewhat similar to the one still practiced in many North African communities to this day (found here) along with six — count ’em, six! — questions asked by the eldest (‽) child to the father.

Note: For clarity, we have replace the use of the Solidus (a/k/a “forward slash”) as found in the Fraktur script of the printed work, with its equivalent — the comma.

Source (German) Translation (English)
Nachdem er davon getruncken[1] In modern German: getrunken , nimmt er den untersten Kuchen von der ersten Nacht Israel, und bricht denselben mitten von einander/und wickelt den halben Theil[2] In modern German: Teil  in ein reines Hand-Tuch[3] In modern German: Handtuch , und hänget[4] In modern German: hängt  es über die Achseln, als wie einer der reisen will, dabey[5] In modern German: dabei  er sagt:
 
Höret ihr Kinder! so seyn[6] In modern German: sein  unsere Eltern eilends mit einem Stabe[7] In modern German: Stab  und ungesäuertem Teige aus Egypten[8] In modern German: Ägypten  von der Hand Pharao gegangen.
After drinking from [the first cup], he takes the bottom loaf from the first night, Israel,[9] The loaves are referred to in the Jüdisches Ceremoniel as Chan (Kohen), Levi, and Israel. Note that in modern practice the middle matzo is broken, not the bottom one. This is likely a misremembering on the part of Kirchner.  and breaks it in half, and wraps half of it in a clean handtowel, and hangs it over his shoulders, as if one were about to travel, and says:
 
“Hear ye, children! This is how our ancestors hastily, carrying a staff and unleavened dough, went out from the hand of Pharaoh.”
1.) Der älteste von dem Söhnen fragt: Vater, warum sind sie denn heraus gangen, wie du uns vorweisest?
 
Darauf der Vater antwortet: Pharao drückte uns mit schwerer Arbeit, gab uns Wasser und Brodt,[10] In modern German: Brot  doch ohne Wasser. Nachdem uns aber Gott die Gnade erwieß[11] In modern German: erwies , und uns durch Mosen ließ ausführen, nahm ein jedweder den Teig auf seine Achseln, und liess damit aus Egypten.[12] In modern German: Ägypten 
1.) The eldest[13] Note that the Four Questions are traditionally recited by the youngest son. This is likely another misremembering on the part of Kirchner.  of the sons asks: “Father, why did they go out then, as you showed us?”
 
After which the father answers: “Pharaoh oppressed us with hard labor, gave us water and bread, but without water. But after God showed us grace and allowed us to be led out by Moses, each of us took dough on his shoulders and left Egypt with it.”
2.) Fragt der Sohn: Warum essen wir das Grüne von dem Rettig[14] In modern German: Rettich , sampt[15] In modern German: samt  den Aepffeln,[16] In modern German: Äpfeln  Nüssen und Zummet?[17] In modern German: Zimt 
 
Darauf der Vater antwortet: Unsere Eltern haben Stroh müssen hacken unter den Leim den gantzen[18] In modern German: ganzen  Tag, und bedeutet das Grüne von Meerrettig[19] In modern German: Meerrettich  das Stroh, das andere den Leim.
2.) The son asks: “Why do we eat the green of the radish, along with the apples, nuts, and cinnamon?”
 
The father replies: “Our parents had to chop straw under plaster all day, and the green of the horseradish represents the straw, the other part the plaster.”
3.) [Fragt der Sohn:] Warum essen wir denn den Meerrettig?[20] In modern German: Meerrettich 
 
Der Vater antwortet: Die Arbeit ist uns bitter ankommen, wie auch unser Brodt[21] In modern German: Brot , darum essen wir Meerrettig.[22] In modern German: Meerrettich 
3.) [The son asks:] “Then why do we eat the horseradish?”
 
The father answers: “The work came bitterly upon us, as did the bread, so we eat horseradish.”
4.) [Fragt der Sohn:] Warum essen wir Petersilge[23] In modern German: Petersilie  in Saltz-Wasser[24] In modern German: Salzwasser  eingetunckt[25] In modern German: eingetunkt  oder in Eßig?[26] In modern German: Essig 
 
Der Vater sagt: Weil wir damahls[27] In modern German: damals  nichts zu essen gehabt, als Graß[28] In modern German: Gras , das wir mit Eßig[29] In modern German: Essig  angemacht.
4.) [The son asks:] “Why do we eat parsley dipped in saltwater or vinegar?”
 
The father says: “Because back then we had nothing to eat but grass, which we dressed with vinegar.”
5.) [Fragt der Sohn:] Warum essen wir Eyer?[30] In modern German: Eier  Darauff[31] In modern German: Darauf 
 
antwortet der Vater: Wanne[32] In modern German: Wenn  einer von unsern Eltern gestorben, haben wir denselben Tag nicht dürffen[33] In modern German: dürfen  kochen; sondern es haben uns Frembde[34] In modern German: Fremde  ein wenig weisses[35] In modern German (excluding Switzerland): weißes  Brodt,[36] In modern German: Brot  Asche und Eyer[37] In modern German: Eier  überschicket,[38] In modern German: überschickt  und wir gedenken an die blutsaure und schwere Todes-Arbeit.[39] In modern German: Todesarbeit 
5.) [The son asks:] “Why do we eat eggs?”
 
After which the father answers: “When one of our ancestors died, we would not be allowed to cook on that same day; instead, strangers sent us a little white bread, ashes, and eggs, and we would commemorate the bloody sour and arduous death-work.”
6.) [Fragt der Sohn:] Warum liegt auf der Schüssel ein Stück gebraten Fleisch?
 
[Der Vater antwortet:] Weil man das Oster-Lamm[40] In modern German: Osterlamm  meistentheils[41] In modern German: meistenteils  gebraten.
6.) [The son asks:] “Why is there a piece of roasted meat upon the dish?”
 
[The father answers:] “Because the paschal lamb[42] Literally: “Easter lamb.” Uncommon in modern German usage, at the time both Passover and Easter were referred to as such. This is still the case in many languages — see Greek Πάσχα των Ιουδαίων, French Pâque juive, Finnish juutalainen pääsiäinen, and Spanish pascua de los judíos. Note, though, that in all three of those languages, the word for Easter derives from the Hebrew פֶּסַח via Aramaic, which is not the case in German — or in English.  was usually roasted.”

Source(s)

Jüdisches Ceremoniel (Paul Christian Kirchner, 1717), p. 21

Jüdisches Ceremoniel (Paul Christian Kirchner, 1717), p. 22

Note, though, that in all three of those languages, the word for Easter derives from the Hebrew פֶּסַח via Aramaic, which is not the case in German — or in English.
